metadata.description: How authorization works for Alation Agent Studio: identity from the integration through to data in the warehouse.

presentation._doc: Presentation layer for auth_flow_explorer.html. The HTML renders ONLY from here + the model arrays above; it holds no content of its own. min_level: 0=exec 1=user 2=legal 3=security (additive). Move-verbatim from the former HTML constants.

personas.label: Executive
personas.blurb: the shape & the one identity break

personas.label: User
personas.blurb: what each role can actually do

personas.label: Security
personas.blurb: LLM hosting and the auth model

platforms.label: Slack
platforms.blurb: Slack verifies the request signature, exchanges a per-user OAuth token, and goes through a published agent. It does not call tools or the API directly.

platforms.label: Claude / Codex plugin
platforms.blurb: The on-device plugin runs per-user OAuth and can take any of three paths: chat with a published agent, invoke a tool directly, or call the REST API. All carry the same per-user token.

platforms.label: MCP
platforms.blurb: An MCP client can take any of three paths over /ai/mcp: a published agent (surfaced as a tool), a tool directly, or the API. The same per-user token is verified and carried downstream.

dp_support.rule: Each person who uses Data Product Chat or an agent connects with their own account on the underlying database, and needs read access to the relevant tables.

decision.title: Which identity runs the query?
decision.intro: Agents and Chat with Data work the same way.

steps.q: Has this person connected their own account for the data product?
steps.note: Set up in the Data Products UI, where the user connects their own account to the data product.

steps.q: Has an admin enabled a shared connection account for this data product?
steps.note: An optional setup an admin turns on; it is not the default.

outcome_user.label: Runs as the person's own account
outcome_user.body: The query runs as that person on the database, so the warehouse applies their own access rights to the results. This is the default.

outcome_sa.label: Runs as a shared connection account
outcome_sa.body: Everyone querying this data product connects with one shared account that an admin set up. Used only when that option is turned on.

outcome_fail.label: The query doesn't run
outcome_fail.body: If the person has not connected their own account and no shared account is set up, the query simply doesn't run — it never borrows someone else's access.

signin.note: per-user; what every Agent Studio integration uses

frontdoor.title: Incoming request
frontdoor.sub: pick the platform the request comes from
frontdoor.plain: A request comes in from a supported platform and runs through a per-user OAuth flow, so every later step acts as that specific person. The platform decides where the request can enter: at a published agent, or directly at tools and APIs.
frontdoor.know: All platforms share the same per-user OAuth. What differs is the entry point: Slack always goes through a published agent, while the plugin and MCP can also invoke tools or APIs directly, skipping the agent wrapper.

tech.g: Every platform carries a per-user Alation OAuth token; none uses a shared bot identity on the data path.
tech.j: U2M OAuth (Authorization-Code + PKCE), per-user bearer to /ai/api/v1/chats or /ai/mcp.

signin.title: Signs in as themselves
signin.sub: Their own Alation account, not a shared bot
signin.plain: The person connects their own Alation account, so every later step acts as that specific person.
signin.know: Their real Alation identity now travels with the request.

tech.g: A one-time browser login links the Slack user to their Alation account.
tech.j: OAuth Authorization-Code + PKCE; a per-user token is minted and replayed downstream.

agent.title: AI agent (Agent Studio)
agent.sub: the entry point, a published agent
agent.plain: The whole conversation runs inside an AI agent built in Agent Studio. The agent is the entry point: the person talks to it, and it decides which of its tools to call.
agent.know: The first authorization question: is this person allowed to use this agent at all?

tech.g: Published agents are visible to everyone; drafts only to their author. Role is NOT a threshold here.
tech.j: Visibility filter on the agent's published status and owner.

facets.label: Which LLM runs the agent
facets.body: By default the agent runs on an Alation-hosted AWS Bedrock model (Claude Sonnet/Opus/Haiku), routed by AWS region. Tenants can Bring Your Own Model: a per-feature or per-agent override falls back to a tenant default, then to the Alation default. BYOM supports Bedrock and OpenAI / Anthropic / OpenAI-compatible providers via API key + base URL. LLM credentials are encrypted at rest.

tools.title: The agent’s tools
tools.sub: a fixed set chosen by the agent’s author
tools.plain: Each agent carries a fixed set of tools picked by its author. It can only call those; it can’t invent new powers at runtime.
tools.know: Tools come in two kinds, and they reach data in two very different ways (see the split below).

tech.g: Tools inherit the parent agent’s visibility — there is no extra per-user data-permission check at the tool layer itself.
tech.j: Tool authorization checks the parent agent's visibility; there are no extra scopes at the tool layer.

tool_cat.title: Reads data in Alation
tool_cat.plain: This kind of tool reads data that lives inside Alation itself: table & column descriptions, lineage, glossary terms, and sample values stored in the catalog.
tool_cat.know: It never touches the warehouse. What comes back is governed by your catalog permissions and your role.

tech.g: Returns catalog context scoped to the caller’s grants.

tool_dp.title: Queries a data product
tool_dp.sub: a governed gateway to the warehouse
tool_dp.plain: This kind of tool answers questions that need real data by going through a data product: a governed, published query interface over a warehouse table.
tool_dp.know: The data product must be shared with you (or granted) first. Only then is the query sent onward to the warehouse.

warehouse.title: Runs the query on the warehouse
warehouse.plain: The approved query runs against the real warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, …) and rows come back.
warehouse.know: Which rows/columns you get depends on which credential the warehouse sees, set at the ⚠ boundary just above.

tech.g: own ACTIVE → shared ACTIVE → own any-status → shared any-status → None. A broken own credential does NOT silently fall through to the shared SA.

infra.label: AWS Bedrock
infra.plain: The agent's default model runs on Alation-hosted AWS Bedrock (Claude), with the region chosen per request — and it hard-fails in production if the region is unset, to honor data residency.

infra.label: AWS STS / IAM roles
infra.sub: no static keys on the default path
infra.plain: Bedrock and S3 access use workload IAM roles via AWS STS, so no static AWS keys are stored on the default path.

infra.label: Envelope encryption
infra.plain: LLM/BYOM credentials, OAuth secrets, and chat content are encrypted at rest with per-tenant envelope encryption.

infra.label: Token service
infra.plain: Mints a short-lived (15-minute) JWT carrying the end-user's identity, used by the Zero-Data gateway.

infra.label: Session validation
infra.plain: On the browser path, the user's session is validated and resolved to their tenant identity.

infra.label: Credential resolution
infra.plain: Resolves which stored warehouse credential runs a query for this person and data product.

bounds.label: Is it really from Slack?
bounds.title: Boundary · message authenticity
bounds.plain: We confirm the message genuinely came from Slack and wasn’t forged or replayed.

tech.g: Slack signs every request; we check that signature before doing anything.
tech.j: HMAC signature verified with the app signing secret.

bounds.label: Proven identity travels
bounds.title: Boundary · verified identity is carried
bounds.plain: The person’s verified Alation identity is passed to every internal service; nothing acts on their behalf using a shared account.

tech.g: A signed identity token is checked at each service and forwarded on.
tech.j: Tenant JWT verified via JWKS at each service and forwarded downstream.

bounds.label: ⚠ Identity changes hands
bounds.title: Boundary · the warehouse sees a different identity
bounds.plain: Here the person’s Alation login stops. The warehouse is reached with a stored database credential; the Alation token is not forwarded to the warehouse.

tech.g: Alation looks up the best stored credential for this person + data product and connects with that.
tech.j: Stored-credential lookup prefers the user's own credential; a shared service account must be enabled per data product by an admin.

agent.label: Use a published agent
agent.why: Any signed-in user can run agents that have been published.

agent.label: Create / edit a draft agent
agent.why: Authoring needs a Creator-tier license. Viewers & Explorers cannot build agents — only use them.

agent.label: Publish an agent for others
agent.why: Moving an agent from draft → published is a builder capability; consumers can’t publish.

tools.label: Run the agent’s tools
tools.why: Tools are fixed by the author; anyone who can use the agent can run them.

tools.label: Choose / configure tools
tools.why: Only the agent’s author (a builder) decides which tools the agent carries.

dp.label: View / query (granted)
dp.why: Anyone can be granted access to a data product per-object — independent of their global role.

dp.label: Manage product (Product Admin)
dp.why: Editing a data product needs a per-object Product Admin grant — typically held by builders.

dp.label: Full access as Server Admin
dp.why: Server Admin has full access to data products; other roles get access through an explicit grant.
